content:
# Acts Chapter 18 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 247-248 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_018_page_001.jpg, ACT_018_page_002.jpg

---

**1** After this, Paul left Athens and went to Corinth. **2** There he found a Jewish man named Aquila, originally from Pontus, who had recently arrived from Italy with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had ordered all Jews to leave Rome. Paul went to see them, **3** and because they shared the same trade — they were tentmakers — he moved in with them and they worked side by side. **4** Every Sabbath he would argue his case in the synagogue, working to persuade both Jews and Greeks.

**5** When Silas and Timothy arrived from Macedonia, Paul threw himself entirely into preaching, passionately testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah. **6** But when they turned hostile and hurled insults at him, he shook out his garments and told them, "Your blood is on your own heads! I am not responsible. From now on, I am going to the Gentiles."

**7** So he left the synagogue and moved next door, to the house of a man named Titius Justus, a worshiper of God. **8** Crispus, the synagogue leader, put his trust in the Lord along with his entire household, and many of the Corinthians who heard the message believed and were baptized. **9** One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision: "Do not be afraid. Keep speaking and do not be silent, **10** because I am with you, and no one is going to lay a hand on you to harm you, for I have many people in this city." **11** So Paul stayed for a year and six months, teaching the word of God among them.

**12** But when Gallio became proconsul of Achaia, the Jews made a united attack on Paul and hauled him before the tribunal. **13** "This man," they charged, "is persuading people to worship God in ways that violate the law!" **14** Paul was just about to open his mouth when Gallio said to the Jews, "If this were some crime or serious wrongdoing, I would have reason to hear you out. **15** But since this is a dispute about words and names and your own law, deal with it yourselves. I refuse to be a judge of such things." **16** And he had them driven away from the tribunal. **17** Then the crowd seized Sosthenes, the synagogue leader, and beat him right there in front of the tribunal. But Gallio could not have cared less.

**18** Paul stayed on in Corinth for quite some time. Then he said goodbye to the brothers and sisters and sailed for Syria, accompanied by Priscilla and Aquila. At Cenchreae he had his hair cut, because he was under a vow. **19** They arrived at Ephesus, and there Paul left Priscilla and Aquila behind. He himself went into the synagogue and engaged in discussion with the Jews. **20** When they asked him to stay longer, he declined, **21** but as he said farewell he told them, "I will come back to you, God willing." Then he set sail from Ephesus.

**22** After landing at Caesarea, he went up and greeted the church, and then went down to Antioch. **23** He spent some time there, and then set out again, traveling systematically through the region of Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples as he went.

**24** Meanwhile, a Jewish man named Apollos arrived in Ephesus. He was from Alexandria, an eloquent man with a powerful command of the Scriptures. **25** He had been instructed in the Way of the Lord, and with burning enthusiasm he spoke and taught accurately about Jesus — though he knew only the baptism of John. **26** He began to speak boldly in the synagogue. When Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him aside and explained the Way of God to him more accurately. **27** When he wanted to cross over to Achaia, the brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote to the disciples there to welcome him. On arriving, he proved to be a tremendous asset to those who had come to faith through grace, **28** for he vigorously refuted the Jews in public debate, demonstrating from the Scriptures that the Messiah was Jesus.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**On translation approach:** Acts 18 is a chapter of arrivals and departures, courtroom drama, and divine encouragement. I have translated for narrative pace, rendering Paul's emotional arc — from quiet tentmaking to passionate preaching to courtroom confrontation to night-vision comfort — as a story that moves.

**Verse 2 — "originally from Pontus":** The Greek *Pontikon to genei* is a geographic-ethnic identifier. "Originally from Pontus" conveys the biographical layering Luke intends: Aquila is a Pontian Jew now displaced from Rome to Corinth. The historical resonance of Claudius's expulsion edict (c. 49 CE) shapes this whole scene.

**Verse 5 — "threw himself entirely into preaching":** Sinaiticus reads *synecheto to logo* — Paul was "constrained" or "gripped" by the word. The dynamic rendering captures the intensity: the arrival of his co-workers freed Paul from tentmaking to devote himself fully to proclamation.

**Verse 6 — "shook out his garments":** The Greek *ektinaxamenos ta himatia* is a prophetic gesture of renunciation (cf. Nehemiah 5:13). I have kept the physical action visible rather than abstracting it, because the bodily drama matters — this is a public break.

**Verse 7 — "Titius Justus":** Sinaiticus reads *Titiou Ioustou*. Some manuscripts give "Titus Justus." The name carries Roman overtones — Paul is literally moving next door from Jewish space into a Roman-named household, a geographic metaphor for his Gentile turn.

**Verse 14-15 — Gallio's dismissal:** Gallio's speech is a masterpiece of Roman bureaucratic contempt. I have rendered it with the clipped, impatient tone of a magistrate who considers the case beneath him. "Deal with it yourselves" captures the force of the Greek dismissal.

**Verse 17 — "Gallio could not have cared less":** The Greek *ouden touton to Gallio emelen* is often softened to "Gallio paid no attention." But the Greek *emelen* (it mattered) with the negation is emphatic indifference. The dynamic rendering preserves the narrator's wry observation about Roman disregard for Jewish internal conflicts.

**Verse 18 — "had his hair cut, because he was under a vow":** The Nazirite vow reference is ambiguous in the Greek — the participle could grammatically attach to either Paul or Aquila, though Paul is the more natural subject. I have followed the majority reading. The vow signals Paul's continued Jewish observance even as he turns to Gentile mission.

**Verse 25 — "burning enthusiasm":** The Greek *zeon to pneumati* (boiling in spirit) describes Apollos's passionate intensity. "Burning enthusiasm" captures the thermal metaphor while connecting to the Spirit dimension of his teaching.

**Verse 27 — "a tremendous asset":** The Greek *synebaleto poly* means "contributed greatly." Rather than the flat "was a great help," I have rendered the force of Apollos's impact — he was not merely helpful but transformative for the believing community.

**Verse 28 — "vigorously refuted":** The Greek *eutonos* means "with full force" or "strenuously." Apollos did not politely disagree — he overwhelmed his opponents with scriptural argument. The public nature (*demosiai*) of this debate matters: this was forensic rhetoric in the civic square.

— *PNEUMA*
